main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Preference-Attended Multi-Objective Decision Transformer (PA-MODT): a transformer-based architecture with a preference-attention block and modular structure for offline multi-objective RL"],"problems":["Scalability of offline multi-objective RL algorithms across large preference spaces","Handling unknown preferences during evaluation in offline MORL","Weak correlation between preferences and predictions in transformer-based MORL models","Inefficient model utilization and limited flexibility in preference prediction for existing transformer architectures"],"datasets":["D4MORL datasets (five variants, including MO-Ant) with millions of trajectories representing various objectives"],"metrics":["Pareto front alignment with behavioral policy","Generalization over observed and unobserved preferences","Sensitivity of evaluation metrics to Pareto front variations"],"results":[{"metric":"Pareto front alignment","value":"PA-MODT achieves Pareto fronts that align closely with behavioral policy and outperform existing models"},{"metric":"Generalization","value":"PA-MODT generalizes effectively over different preferences and trajectories, including unobserved preferences"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of PA-MODT, a novel transformer-based architecture with a preference-attention block for offline MORL","PA-MODT enables effective generalization across large preference spaces and trajectories","Demonstration that PA-MODT outperforms existing models (including MORvS) on large-scale offline MORL datasets","Comprehensive analysis of evaluation metric sensitivity to Pareto front variations in MORL"]}

methods: Preference-Attended Multi-Objective Decision Transformer (PA-MODT): a transformer-based architecture with a preference-attention block and modular structure for offline multi-objective RL
problems: Scalability of offline multi-objective RL algorithms across large preference spaces
Handling unknown preferences during evaluation in offline MORL
Weak correlation between preferences and predictions in transformer-based MORL models
Inefficient model utilization and limited flexibility in preference prediction for existing transformer architectures
datasets: D4MORL datasets (five variants, including MO-Ant) with millions of trajectories representing various objectives
metrics: Pareto front alignment with behavioral policy
Generalization over observed and unobserved preferences
Sensitivity of evaluation metrics to Pareto front variations

results.value: PA-MODT achieves Pareto fronts that align closely with behavioral policy and outperform existing models
PA-MODT generalizes effectively over different preferences and trajectories, including unobserved preferences

novelty_claims: Introduction of PA-MODT, a novel transformer-based architecture with a preference-attention block for offline MORL
PA-MODT enables effective generalization across large preference spaces and trajectories
Demonstration that PA-MODT outperforms existing models (including MORvS) on large-scale offline MORL datasets
Comprehensive analysis of evaluation metric sensitivity to Pareto front variations in MORL

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Pareto-Efficient Decision Agents (PEDA): a family of offline multi-objective RL algorithms extending Decision Transformers via a novel preference-and-return-conditioned policy network","Supervised learning loss for training preference and return conditioned policy networks"],"problems":["Learning preference-agnostic policies for multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL) in offline settings","Generalizing to arbitrary user preferences at test time using only offline demonstrations","Lack of large-scale, high-quality offline datasets for MORL benchmarking"],"datasets":["D4MORL: Datasets for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning, consisting of 1.8 million trajectories on 6 MuJoCo environments (5 with 2 objectives, 1 with 3 objectives), with expert and amateur demonstrations and 3 preference distributions"],"metrics":["Hypervolume","Sparsity"],"results":[{"metric":"Hypervolume (HalfCheetah, Expert)","value":"5.77 × 10^6"},{"metric":"Hypervolume (HalfCheetah, Amateur)","value":"5.76 × 10^6"},{"metric":"Hypervolume (HalfCheetah, Behavioral Policy Upper Bound)","value":"5.79 × 10^6"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of D4MORL, the first large-scale offline dataset specifically designed for multi-objective RL, enabling benchmarking in high-dimensional environments with multiple objectives and preference distributions.","Proposal of Pareto-Efficient Decision Agents (PEDA), a new family of offline MORL algorithms that extend return-conditioned methods (like Decision Transformers) to the multi-objective setting via a novel preference-and-return-conditioned policy network.","Demonstration that PEDA can closely approximate the Pareto-frontier of the behavioral policy using only offline data, achieving near-expert performance as measured by hypervolume."]}

methods: Pareto-Efficient Decision Agents (PEDA): a family of offline multi-objective RL algorithms extending Decision Transformers via a novel preference-and-return-conditioned policy network
Supervised learning loss for training preference and return conditioned policy networks
problems: Learning preference-agnostic policies for multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL) in offline settings
Generalizing to arbitrary user preferences at test time using only offline demonstrations
Lack of large-scale, high-quality offline datasets for MORL benchmarking
datasets: D4MORL: Datasets for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning, consisting of 1.8 million trajectories on 6 MuJoCo environments (5 with 2 objectives, 1 with 3 objectives), with expert and amateur demonstrations and 3 preference distributions
novelty_claims: Introduction of D4MORL, the first large-scale offline dataset specifically designed for multi-objective RL, enabling benchmarking in high-dimensional environments with multiple objectives and preference distributions.

Proposal of Pareto-Efficient Decision Agents (PEDA), a new family of offline MORL algorithms that extend return-conditioned methods (like Decision Transformers) to the multi-objective setting via a novel preference-and-return-conditioned policy network.
Demonstration that PEDA can closely approximate the Pareto-frontier of the behavioral policy using only offline data, achieving near-expert performance as measured by hypervolume.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Multi-Objective Decision Transformer (MO-DT): a Decision Transformer variant trained with multi-objective optimization to predict actions, states, and returns using transformer architecture with multiple attention heads and Gaussian prediction heads for states and returns.","Trust Region Decision Transformer (MO-TRDT): an extension of MO-DT that models conditional distributions over broader action space regions (action regions) to reduce reliance on the behavioral policy and improve generalization."],"problems":["Ineffective utilization of the transformer attention mechanism in standard Decision Transformer (DT) due to single-task (action prediction) training.","Potential inaccuracies in trajectory representation for sequence modeling in offline RL, especially due to non-smooth action distributions from the behavioral policy.","Over-reliance of learned policies on the behavioral policy in offline RL, limiting generalization."],"datasets":["D4RL benchmark locomotion tasks (including gym datasets such as walker2d)"],"metrics":["Average return (normalized score) on D4RL benchmark tasks"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance on D4RL locomotion tasks","value":"MO-DT and MO-TRDT match or outperform current state-of-the-art methods and substantially outperform vanilla Decision Transformer (DT) (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Reformulating offline RL as a multi-objective optimization problem within the Decision Transformer framework, enabling simultaneous prediction of actions, states, and returns.","Introducing MO-DT, which leverages multi-objective optimization and enhanced attention mechanisms (multiple heads, Gaussian prediction heads) for improved performance in offline RL.","Proposing MO-TRDT, which models conditional distributions over action space regions, reducing policy dependence on the behavioral policy and improving generalization.","Demonstrating that these approaches allow for more effective use of the transformer's attention mechanism and achieve performance competitive with or superior to state-of-the-art methods on D4RL benchmarks."]}

methods: Multi-Objective Decision Transformer (MO-DT): a Decision Transformer variant trained with multi-objective optimization to predict actions, states, and returns using transformer architecture with multiple attention heads and Gaussian prediction heads for states and returns.

Trust Region Decision Transformer (MO-TRDT): an extension of MO-DT that models conditional distributions over broader action space regions (action regions) to reduce reliance on the behavioral policy and improve generalization.
problems: Ineffective utilization of the transformer attention mechanism in standard Decision Transformer (DT) due to single-task (action prediction) training.
Potential inaccuracies in trajectory representation for sequence modeling in offline RL, especially due to non-smooth action distributions from the behavioral policy.
Over-reliance of learned policies on the behavioral policy in offline RL, limiting generalization.
datasets: D4RL benchmark locomotion tasks (including gym datasets such as walker2d)
metrics: Average return (normalized score) on D4RL benchmark tasks

results.value: MO-DT and MO-TRDT match or outperform current state-of-the-art methods and substantially outperform vanilla Decision Transformer (DT) (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: Reformulating offline RL as a multi-objective optimization problem within the Decision Transformer framework, enabling simultaneous prediction of actions, states, and returns.
Introducing MO-DT, which leverages multi-objective optimization and enhanced attention mechanisms (multiple heads, Gaussian prediction heads) for improved performance in offline RL.
Proposing MO-TRDT, which models conditional distributions over action space regions, reducing policy dependence on the behavioral policy and improving generalization.

Demonstrating that these approaches allow for more effective use of the transformer's attention mechanism and achieve performance competitive with or superior to state-of-the-art methods on D4RL benchmarks.

methods: Multi-agent Decision Transformer (MADT): a transformer-based architecture for multi-agent offline reinforcement learning, casting MARL as conditional sequence modeling
Offline pre-training of MADT on large-scale offline MARL datasets
Online fine-tuning of pre-trained MADT for improved sample efficiency and generalization
Construction of the first large-scale offline MARL dataset with diverse quality levels based on the StarCraft II environment
problems: Lack of offline pre-training and online fine-tuning paradigms in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL)
Absence of large-scale offline MARL datasets and benchmarks
Difficulty in generalizing policies across different agents and tasks in MARL
Sample inefficiency and high computational cost in MARL due to limited data and expensive online interactions
Out-of-distribution and training paradigm mismatch problems in offline-to-online MARL transfer
datasets: StarCraft II Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) offline dataset (newly constructed with diverse skill levels and scenario variations)
metrics: Sample efficiency (number of environment steps to reach a certain performance)
Generalization to new tasks (few-shot and zero-shot performance)

results.value: MADT outperforms state-of-the-art offline RL baselines (BCQ, CQL)
Pre-trained MADT significantly improves sample efficiency compared to baselines
MADT demonstrates strong performance in both few-shot and zero-shot transfer to new tasks

novelty_claims: First work to study and demonstrate the effectiveness of offline pre-trained models for multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) in terms of sample efficiency and generalizability
Introduction of the first large-scale offline MARL dataset with diverse quality levels based on StarCraft II
Proposal of the Multi-agent Decision Transformer (MADT), a novel transformer-based architecture for offline and online MARL
Demonstration that a single pre-trained sequence model can solve multiple SMAC tasks and transfer between different types of agents and scenarios

methods: Max-Entropy enhanced Decision Transformer with Reward Relabeling (EDT4Rec)
Max-entropy exploration strategy inspired by Soft Actor-Critic (SAC)
Reward relabeling strategy for stitching sub-optimal trajectories
problems: Data inefficiency in RL-based recommender systems due to limited user interactions
Lack of stitching capability in Decision Transformer-based recommendation methods (inability to learn from sub-optimal trajectories)

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["MOPO (Model-based Offline Policy Optimization): a model-based offline RL algorithm that penalizes the reward function using the predicted variance (uncertainty) of a Lipschitz-regularized dynamics model, optimizing policy in an uncertainty-penalized MDP.","Modification of MBPO (Model-Based Policy Optimization) to include a reward penalty based on model error estimates, allowing generalization to out-of-distribution states and actions."],"problems":["Offline reinforcement learning: learning policies from a fixed batch of previously collected data without online exploration.","Distributional shift between the offline training data and the learned policy, leading to extrapolation errors and unstable learning.","Generalization beyond the support of the offline data, i.e., learning effective policies for states and actions not well represented in the dataset."],"datasets":["D4RL benchmark (a standard offline RL benchmark)","Two challenging continuous control tasks requiring generalization to out-of-distribution states (not named explicitly in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Return (cumulative reward) in the true MDP"],"results":[{"metric":"Return (D4RL benchmark and out-of-distribution tasks)","value":"MOPO substantially outperforms both standard model-based RL (MBPO) and state-of-the-art model-free offline RL approaches."}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new model-based offline RL algorithm (MOPO) that penalizes the reward function using the predicted variance of a Lipschitz-regularized model, enabling generalization beyond the data support.","Theoretical guarantee that optimizing in the uncertainty-penalized MDP maximizes a lower bound of the return in the true MDP, providing a principled trade-off between return and risk.","Empirical demonstration that MOPO outperforms both model-free and standard model-based offline RL methods on standard benchmarks and tasks requiring out-of-distribution generalization."]}

methods: MOPO (Model-based Offline Policy Optimization): a model-based offline RL algorithm that penalizes the reward function using the predicted variance (uncertainty) of a Lipschitz-regularized dynamics model, optimizing policy in an uncertainty-penalized MDP.
Modification of MBPO (Model-Based Policy Optimization) to include a reward penalty based on model error estimates, allowing generalization to out-of-distribution states and actions.
problems: Offline reinforcement learning: learning policies from a fixed batch of previously collected data without online exploration.
Distributional shift between the offline training data and the learned policy, leading to extrapolation errors and unstable learning.
Generalization beyond the support of the offline data, i.e., learning effective policies for states and actions not well represented in the dataset.
datasets: D4RL benchmark (a standard offline RL benchmark)
Two challenging continuous control tasks requiring generalization to out-of-distribution states (not named explicitly in the provided text)
metrics: Return (cumulative reward) in the true MDP

results.value: MOPO substantially outperforms both standard model-based RL (MBPO) and state-of-the-art model-free offline RL approaches.

novelty_claims: Proposes a new model-based offline RL algorithm (MOPO) that penalizes the reward function using the predicted variance of a Lipschitz-regularized model, enabling generalization beyond the data support.
Theoretical guarantee that optimizing in the uncertainty-penalized MDP maximizes a lower bound of the return in the true MDP, providing a principled trade-off between return and risk.
Empirical demonstration that MOPO outperforms both model-free and standard model-based offline RL methods on standard benchmarks and tasks requiring out-of-distribution generalization.

methods: Extension of offline policy-regularized methods to multi-objective RL (MORL)
Filtering out preference-inconsistent demonstrations via approximating behavior preferences

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Temporal logic Specification-conditioned Decision Transformer (SDT)","Conditioning Decision Transformer (DT) on Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications","Incorporation of prefix and suffix robustness values as input tokens"],"problems":["Offline safe reinforcement learning with complex temporal and logical constraints","Learning policies from fixed datasets that satisfy temporal logic constraints and maximize reward","Generalizing to different degrees of satisfaction of temporal logic specifications without retraining"],"datasets":["DSRL benchmarks"],"metrics":["Safety (constraint satisfaction)","Task performance (reward)","Robustness value thresholds (degree of STL specification satisfaction)"],"results":[{"metric":"Safety and task performance","value":"SDT outperforms multiple baselines by a large margin"},{"metric":"Generalization to robustness thresholds","value":"SDT can generalize to different robustness value thresholds and configurations without retraining"}],"novelty_claims":["SDT is the first framework to incorporate Signal Temporal Logic (STL) to satisfy temporal constraints in offline safe RL settings.","SDT enables conditioning on STL specifications in Decision Transformer (DT).","SDT introduces prefix and suffix robustness values as input tokens to leverage different portions of a trajectory.","SDT demonstrates superior performance in both safety and task performance compared to existing approaches.","SDT can generalize to different robustness value thresholds and configurations without retraining."]}

Conditioning Decision Transformer (DT) on Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications
Incorporation of prefix and suffix robustness values as input tokens
problems: Offline safe reinforcement learning with complex temporal and logical constraints
Learning policies from fixed datasets that satisfy temporal logic constraints and maximize reward
Generalizing to different degrees of satisfaction of temporal logic specifications without retraining

results.value: SDT outperforms multiple baselines by a large margin
SDT can generalize to different robustness value thresholds and configurations without retraining

novelty_claims: SDT is the first framework to incorporate Signal Temporal Logic (STL) to satisfy temporal constraints in offline safe RL settings.
SDT enables conditioning on STL specifications in Decision Transformer (DT).
SDT introduces prefix and suffix robustness values as input tokens to leverage different portions of a trajectory.
SDT demonstrates superior performance in both safety and task performance compared to existing approaches.
SDT can generalize to different robustness value thresholds and configurations without retraining.

methods: Conservative Q-Learning (CQL): a method that regularizes Q-values to learn conservative, lower-bound estimates of the value function for offline RL
problems: Overestimation of value functions in offline RL due to distributional shift between dataset and learned policy
Poor performance of standard off-policy RL methods in offline settings, especially with complex and multi-modal data distributions
Bootstrapping from out-of-distribution actions and overfitting in offline RL
datasets: Discrete and continuous control domains (specific datasets not named in the provided text)
Datasets with complex and multi-modal data distributions
Realistic datasets collected from human interaction

results.value: CQL attains 2-5 times higher final return than prior offline RL methods on many benchmark tasks

novelty_claims: Proposes Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), a new algorithmic framework for learning conservative, lower-bound Q-functions in offline RL
Theoretical guarantee that the expected value of the learned Q-function under the policy lower-bounds the true policy value
CQL is simple to implement, requiring less than 20 lines of code added to standard RL algorithms
CQL is robust to Q-function estimation error and outperforms prior offline RL methods, especially on complex and multi-modal datasets and high-dimensional visual input domains
CQL is the only method that can outperform behavioral cloning on several realistic, human-collected datasets

methods: Diffusion Q-learning (Diffusion-QL): representing the policy as a conditional diffusion model (denoising diffusion probabilistic model, DDPM)
Learning an action-value function (Q-function) and incorporating a policy improvement term into the diffusion model's training loss
Policy regularization via diffusion models, combining behavior cloning and Q-learning guidance in the reverse diffusion process
problems: Offline reinforcement learning: learning optimal policies from static, previously collected datasets without environment interaction
Poor performance of standard RL methods in offline settings due to function approximation errors on out-of-distribution actions
Limited expressiveness of policy classes (e.g., Gaussian policies) leading to suboptimal solutions and poor policy regularization
Inadequate regularization methods (e.g., KL divergence, MMD) for offline RL

metrics: Task-specific performance metrics on D4RL (e.g., normalized score)
Performance on simple bandit task (e.g., expected reward)

results.value: State-of-the-art performance; outperforms prior methods on most tasks
Demonstrates superiority over prior methods in handling multimodal behavior policies

novelty_claims: Proposing the use of diffusion models (DDPMs) as highly expressive policy classes for offline RL, capable of modeling complex, multimodal action distributions
Introducing Diffusion-QL, which combines behavior cloning and Q-learning guidance within the diffusion model's training and sampling process
Demonstrating that diffusion model-based policy regularization is more effective than previous methods, leading to state-of-the-art results on D4RL benchmarks
Providing a new perspective on policy regularization in offline RL by leveraging the iterative refinement and strong distribution matching properties of diffusion models

CDT can dynamically adjust the trade-off between safety and task performance at deployment without retraining or fine-tuning.
CDT outperforms strong offline safe RL baselines and its own variants in both safety and task performance across all tasks with the same hyperparameters.
CDT generalizes to different cost thresholds without retraining, unlike prior methods.

COMBO achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard offline RL benchmarks, especially in tasks requiring out-of-distribution generalization

Cal-QL is implemented as a simple modification to Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), calibrating the value function against a reference policy (e.g., the behavior policy)."],"problems":["Inefficiency and poor performance of existing offline RL methods during online fine-tuning after pre-training.","The 'unlearning' phenomenon where conservative offline RL methods degrade policy performance during online fine-tuning due to miscalibrated value estimates.","Need for a method that provides both effective offline policy initialization and efficient online fine-tuning with limited interaction."],"datasets":["Benchmark tasks from [10], [51], and [44], including robotic manipulation and navigation tasks. methods: Calibrated Q-learning (Cal-QL): a method for learning a conservative and calibrated value function for offline RL pre-training to enable efficient online fine-tuning. Cal-QL is implemented as a simple modification to Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), calibrating the value function against a reference policy (e.g., the behavior policy).
problems: Inefficiency and poor performance of existing offline RL methods during online fine-tuning after pre-training.
The 'unlearning' phenomenon where conservative offline RL methods degrade policy performance during online fine-tuning due to miscalibrated value estimates.
Need for a method that provides both effective offline policy initialization and efficient online fine-tuning with limited interaction.
datasets: Benchmark tasks from [10], [51], and [44], including robotic manipulation and navigation tasks. (Exact dataset/task names not specified in the provided text.)

results.metric: Number of fine-tuning benchmark tasks outperformed

results.value: 30–40% on some tasks

novelty_claims: Introduction of Cal-QL, a method for learning calibrated conservative value functions for offline RL that enable efficient online fine-tuning.

Formalization of the calibration property for value functions: providing a lower bound on the true value of the learned policy and an upper bound on the value of a reference policy (e.g., the behavior policy).
Demonstration that calibrating value functions in this way avoids the 'unlearning' phenomenon and leads to better sample efficiency and lower cumulative regret during online fine-tuning.
Cal-QL can be implemented as a simple modification to existing conservative Q-learning methods, requiring only a one-line code change and no additional hyperparameters.
